posts.name: Ryan Caughey
posts.cooked:
Inspired by an off-topic discussion in Sarah Kirschbaum’s Ramonet/Raveneau thread, I pose a simple question: what is the best sub-$100 white Burg?

My tentative vote: Buisson Charles Meursault ‘Gouttes d’Or”

Honorable mention: Bouchard Perrieres

At one time it would have been Boillot’s Puligny Clos de la Mouchere but that’s now an $100+ wine.

I’m interested in everyone’s thoughts.

1)Dauvissat Chablis(just the AOC bottling)

  2. Walter Scott Ex Novo

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked:
Tough question given how much pricing can vary. For example Dauvissat Forest and Raveneau MdT are available to some for well under US$100 but probably not as a rule. Pricing has also accelerated quite a bit, so what was under $100 last year may be no longer! I will put a couple of candidates out there, probably large houses from CdB for “proper” white burg are the best bet as as chances are they will still be under $100 next week, but there’s tremendous quality coming from regions now too:

  • Domaine Chartron Puligny Montrachet 1er Cru Clos du Cailleret
  • Bouchard Meursault 1er Cru Perrieres
  • Drouhin Beaune “Clos de Mouches”
  • Jules Desjourneys top Pouilly Fuisse cuvees (Vignes Blanches and Vignes de la Cote)
  • Guffens Heynen top Pouilly Fuisse cuvees depending on vintage and mood

posts.cooked:

Disclaimer: I import the wine into Australia.

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked:
Domaine Jean-Marc Vincent Santenay 1er Cru ‘Le Beaurepaire’ Blanc

Disclaimer: I import the wine into Australia.

You’d get no argument from me, Jeremy, and it’s $50 here. And, you could add his Santenay 1er cru Gravieres and 1er Auxey-Duresses Les Haute. And, if this were about reds, you could say the same for those.

Others:

Cherisey Meursault Blagny 1er cru La Genelotte
Cherisey Puligny-Montrachet 1er cru Hameau de Blagny
Jean-Marc Pillot Chassagne-Montrachet 1er cru Les Caillerets
Jean-Marc Pillot Chassagne-Montrachet 1er cru Les Vergers Clos St. Marc (really old vines; 2017 will stop you in your tracks; has just how nudged over $100)
Bachelet-Monnot Puligny-Montrachet 1er cru Les Referts
Bachelet-Monnot Puligny-Montrachet 1er cru Les Folatieres
Rollin Pernand-Vergelesses 1er cru Sous Fretille (hard to distinguish from Corton-Charlemagne and it’s closer to $50 than $100)

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked: My vote right now would be with Marty. Of those on his list I would pick the Cherisey Meursault Blagny 1er Genelotte. This is a consistent winner and still priced well. BTW Marty, The 17 JM Pillot Clos St Marc is incredible. The Niellon Chassagne Chaume Clos des Truffieres would be way up there too but the price has now pierced $100.

posts.name: Kent Comley

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: Certainly, I would put Bouchard Meursault Perrieres on the list. A couple of producers not mentioned are Heitz-Lochardet and Christian Moreau

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
There’s honestly still so much, especially if you look beyond Meursault-Puligny-Chassagne (even if those wines also generally remain cheaper ex-domaine than the best domestic Chardonnay from California or Oregon, both DTC and wholesale - indeed, most wines from Coche-Dury, DTC, would be eligible for this thread). But in no particular order:

Bruno Lorenzon Mercurey 1er Cru Pièce 15, Clos des Barraults and Champs Martins
J-M Vincent’s Santenay 1er crus
Dureuil-Janthial Meix Cadot VV and Grésigny
Guffens-Heynen (Verget would dominate a sub-50 list)
Marc Colin Saint-Aubin En Montceau
Lots of bottlings from Olivier Lamy
Lots of bottlings from Pierre-Yves Colin
Jules Desjourneys

With these producers, you are getting first rate winemaking, serious vineyards and great farming, even if the appellations don’t have the most name recognition.

With all of the,

posts.name: Troy Stark
posts.cooked:
A tough question because there are so many different styles of White Burg. For this price point, we tend to seek out Corton-Charlemagne (a few can be found under $100) and highly regarded 1er crus from PM/CM/St. Aubin. We typically don’t find Meursault fits our palate, but obviously lots of people do.

Although, honestly, to us the best value in all of white burg is any good producer making wine from Les Encegnieres/Enseigneres/Ancengnieres (why do they have to spell it so many different ways?!).

posts.name: Lukas Tollig
posts.cooked: Living in Sweden I have really no info on the availability in the US but I’ve had good experience with many whites from Vincent Girardin. They are comparatively fairly priced and a bottle of 2010 Clos du Caillerets I had recently was absolutely faboulous. Bought for about $45-50 back in 2014. Recent vintages are about $90 here in Europe now but might have crept over $100 in the US. But that’s one of the more expensive 1.er crus in their lineup, so there are other options that sell for less.

posts.name: Troy Stark

Not any more!

Mr. Kelley, are you saying they are no longer using lots of new oak on their whites? The last we tried were both from 14; a Meursault Les Charrons and a Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Morgeot. The Meursault was a hot mess. I don’t know how else to describe it. The Morgeot was “fine,” but was over-oaked and missing that tension and chalkiness we’ve come to love in other 14s.

posts.name: Dan Teng
posts.cooked:
1. Walter Scott Ex Novo

…and free shipping and no tax and 10% off case…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Mr. Kelley, are you saying they are no longer using lots of new oak on their whites? The last we tried were both from 14; a Meursault Les Charrons and a Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Morgeot. The Meursault was a hot mess. I don’t know how else to describe it. The Morgeot was “fine,” but was over-oaked and missing that tension and chalkiness we’ve come to love in other 14s.

That is correct. With the 2008 vintage, Eric Germain (who is the brother of Jean-François Germain of Henri Germain in Meursault) began to reduce the percentages of new oak significantly, reduce the toast levels of the new oak they use, extend élevage on the lees, pick earlier and eliminate substandard grape suppliers (i.e. the Bâtard and Montrachet both now come from different sources than in Vincent Girardin’s day). They have also bought more vineyards, and taken over farming some of the vineyards they buy from, working organically and biodynamically. So the style is much racier and now gently reductive. I haven’t had the 2014 Charrons but 2008 Chevalier-Montrachet and 2013 Meursault Charmes showed very well recently, and the 2017s are the best set of wines I ever tasted from them. Try a 2017 Meursault Narvaux and see if you change your mind!

posts.name: Lukas Tollig
posts.cooked:
Living in Sweden I have really no info on the availability in the US but I’ve had good experience with many whites from Vincent Girardin. They are comparatively fairly priced and a bottle of 2010 Clos du Caillerets I had recently was absolutely faboulous. Bought for about $45-50 back in 2014. Recent vintages are about $90 here in Europe now but might have crept over $100 in the US. But that’s one of the more expensive 1.er crus in their lineup, so there are other options that sell for less.

Interesting. My experience with Girardin whites has been the opposite.

I do like some oak and age in my white burgs. I have no experience of Girardin prior to the 2007 vintage, but have not found recent vintages too oaky. The 2010:s are great now, the 2014:s need a few more years for my taste.

posts.name: Adrian Burgess
posts.cooked:
This is my first post and may be a touch off topic, so I apologise for the dubious entrance. However, Jean-Marc Pillot’s Puligny-Montrachet, Les Noyers Brets was mentioned early on, so I thought this may be as good a place as any to ask for some advice. My merchant in the UK has a case of the 2010 for £280 IB - so would work out at only £30 per bottle on the table - this seems a potentially stunning deal !? - I have enjoyed a couple Jean-Marc’s 2008 Chassagne Premier Crus over the last year - drinking perfectly, but in general my preference is Puligny - and I love the 2010 white Burgs I have tried…although they have all been PC / Chablis Grand Cru. Does anyone have any first hand experience of how this wine is drinking ? If not, could someone with more experience give me an idea of how risky a proposition it is ? Primarily, how likely is it to be tired / over-the-hill at age 10? Premox isn’t my main concern as my merchant should refund me on that front, but if it has just faded then that would be on me. Although a great price it is all 12 bottles or nothing, and £367 including taxes would be a significant loss too this wine loving PhD student without any real income…

Very, very much obliged for any tips / insight / guidance

p.s. I have checked CT and there are no reviews since 2013, and I have given up paying too much attention to critics drinking windows as they so rarely tally with my experience

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: Any decent wine with good storage will last more than 8 years after release. I wouldn’t really be concerned about the age. And anything still in bond should have been stored properly from day one. At the moment I happen to be drinking a 2011 table wine that was buried and forgotten in my cellar and is showing surprisingly well considering it was made to be consumed in the first year or two.

posts.name: Adrian Burgess
posts.cooked: Thanks, that is useful, encouraging, and makes sense (please excuse horrible punctuation and spelling in my above post)

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: Envoyer has had some good prices the last few days on Jadot premier crus. I have generally avoided Jadot premier crus because of premox, but the results of Don Cornwell’s recent tastings has made me want to try a couple.

Here’s an opinion albeit biased and lacking a wide range of experience:

Beaune Clos des Mouches

Which was mentioned above

Biased because Joseph is an old friend of the family and I started drinking it at 16 and I’m 63 now. My dad had a personal use import permit - one of only two that JD
imported to in the US at the time. Dad used to import multiple cases of every good year from 1967 until 2007. I remember stealing the occasional bottle out of his cellar and using it as a seduction tool for teenage girls I was after. I always felt it was classier than Quaaludes or coke. It wasn’t too sweet and although dry it had a universal appeal to even the most uneducated American palate - but that should not reflect badly on the quality of the girls I was pursuing.

I’m selling my dad’s collection . He passed on at 97, but I have a bottle of 2007 I’m saving for a special future occasion and we’ll see if what kind of effect it has on a lady my age. LOL

With that kind of “brand loyalty” I have to admit I have tried very few other vintners Burgundies and so not very qualified to opine that Drouhin is the best.

posts.name: C. Keller
posts.cooked:
There’s honestly still so much, especially if you look beyond Meursault-Puligny-Chassagne (even if those wines also generally remain cheaper ex-domaine than the best domestic Chardonnay from California or Oregon, both DTC and wholesale - indeed, most wines from Coche-Dury, DTC, would be eligible for this thread). But in no particular order:

Bruno Lorenzon Mercurey 1er Cru Pièce 15, Clos des Barraults and Champs Martins
J-M Vincent’s Santenay 1er crus
Dureuil-Janthial Meix Cadot VV and Grésigny
Guffens-Heynen (Verget would dominate a sub-50 list)
Marc Colin Saint-Aubin En Montceau
Lots of bottlings from Olivier Lamy
Lots of bottlings from Pierre-Yves Colin
Jules Desjourneys

With these producers, you are getting first rate winemaking, serious vineyards and great farming, even if the appellations don’t have the most name recognition.

With all of the,

Because of this topic I set out on a research mission. Y’all do not help with this (bad) habit. The Janthail was by far the best
2017 Dureuil-Janthial Meix Cadot VV - My goodness. The nose alone made me weak. It was like sticking your nose in a bushel of freshly cut flowers. Great wine all the way thru. Went back for more from First Bottle and they were all cleaned out! And there’s nothing on WineSearcher

2016 Bruno Lorenzon Champs Martin and 2018 Truffieres. Champs was nice and smooth. Recently found a 2018 Truffieres that was just rough and hot.

2016 J-M Santenay Beaurepaire Blanc - Must have been an off bottle. Smelled like wax and never revealed anything on the pallet or nose the entire night it was open. Going back for another to figure this out.

2013 Guffens-Heynen Tri De Chavigne - intense, full bodied monster here. Fruity and floral. Close second to the Janthial

Bongran is on tap next week.

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: Good work Chris - glad you’ve found some you like there.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:

With these producers, you are getting first rate winemaking, serious vineyards and great farming, even if the appellations don’t have the most name recognition.

With all of the,

Because of this topic I set out on a research mission. Y’all do not help with this (bad) habit. The Janthail was by far the best
2017 Dureuil-Janthial Meix Cadot VV - My goodness. The nose alone made me weak. It was like sticking your nose in a bushel of freshly cut flowers. Great wine all the way thru. Went back for more from First Bottle and they were all cleaned out! And there’s nothing on WineSearcher

2016 Bruno Lorenzon Champs Martin and 2018 Truffieres. Champs was nice and smooth. Recently found a 2018 Truffieres that was just rough and hot.

2016 J-M Santenay Beaurepaire Blanc - Must have been an off bottle. Smelled like wax and never revealed anything on the pallet or nose the entire night it was open. Going back for another to figure this out.

2013 Guffens-Heynen Tri De Chavigne - intense, full bodied monster here. Fruity and floral. Close second to the Janthial

Bongran is on tap next week.

2018 Montagny Truffieres on the market already?? That was one 2018 from Lorenzon I didn’t taste, but his 2018s that I did taste turned out very well indeed, reviews are out in a few days. For me, Lorenzon and Dureuil-Janthial are the two greatest estates in the Chalonnaise. Lorenzon’s style for his white wines is a bit more understated, without the sort of praline / nutmeg patina from Chassin barrels that you find in Vincent Dureuil’s wines, but I admire them just as much. Les Croichots is the most open young, whereas Champs Martin and Clos des Barraults have a bit more depth and cut, but are also more tightly wound. His top cuvée in white is the Pièce 15 which is the most multidimensional and complete.

Too bad about the JM Vincent, perhaps just shut down. It was a very tightly wound, acid-driven vintage for him.

Glad you enjoyed the Guffens!

posts.name: Y@0 C <aitch> 00 ng
posts.cooked:
For me, Lorenzon and Dureuil-Janthial are the two greatest estates in the Chalonnaise. Lorenzon’s style for his white wines is a bit more understated, without the sort of praline / nutmeg patina from Chassin barrels that you find in Vincent Dureuil’s wines, but I admire them just as much. Les Croichots is the most open young, whereas Champs Martin and Clos des Barraults have a bit more depth and cut, but are also more tightly wound. His top cuvée in white is the Pièce 15 which is the most multidimensional and complete.

Glad to hear that you think highly of Lorenzon. I took a flyer on the Piece 15 & Champs Martin a little while ago, and it was a pretty good bottle with an intense salty/mineral drive. Would you say that is a characteristic of the Chalonnaise in general, or the producer/bottling? Also would love your thoughts on how long to hold the Piece 15…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Glad to hear that you think highly of Lorenzon. I took a flyer on the Piece 15 & Champs Martin a little while ago, and it was a pretty good bottle with an intense salty/mineral drive. Would you say that is a characteristic of the Chalonnaise in general, or the producer/bottling? Also would love your thoughts on how long to hold the Piece 15…

I would say that, in the best sites, it’s inherently possible but seldom realized. Achieving that effect requires serious viticulture and winemaking; whereas the Côte Chalonnaise has long been subject to a vicious cycle whereby a modest reputation encourages producers to pursue quantity over quality, thereby reinforcing negative perceptions. You probably have to be a bit crazy to put in the sort of work that Bruno has over the last two decades—high density plantings, training the canopies higher, farming organic, long élevage, amazing quality corks, even making his own barrels. And after all of that, the most acknowledgement one is likely to get is “very good for Mercurey”. Yet in a blind tasting vs wines from the same vintage from Puligny, Lorenzon’s emerge as very good with no qualifications. And they really improve with age. For the Pièce 15, the 2010 is still young but showing very well now - to give you an idea.

posts.name: Y@0 C <aitch> 00 ng
posts.cooked: Thank you William! Super insightful

posts.name: Troy Stark
posts.cooked: Agreed. We love Lorenzon’s wines. They’re fantastic (no qualifications here).

posts.cooked:

Nice list to try - Dureuil-Janthial whites are indeed magnificent. I visited last year - he is more the serious / concentrated person when you visit, but super interested in what you have to tell about his wines.

I just bought some Lorenzon 2016s (red) to try - will be first time.

Guffens-Heynen wines I have tried have all been singular (also older ones) - much harder to compare to modern cote de beaune whites than Dureuil-Janthial imho.

Haven’t tried JM Vincent & Bongran (can’t drink everything).

posts.name: Gautier Roussille
posts.cooked:
Ditto on Bruno Lorenzon and Vincent Dureuil-Janthial!
I would also like to mention Didier Larue’s Saint-Aubain 1er Cru Dents de Chien, Claire Naudin-Ferrand Bellis Perennis.
Domaine de Villaine in Bouzeron whites are also one of my go-to bottles in restaurants.

I would have more to say about reds as for whites I mainly drink my wines

posts.name: Gautier Roussille
posts.cooked: Ho and while we’re in The côte Chalonnaise, Jacquesson and David Lefort in Rully…

posts.name: J0zef P1nxten
posts.cooked:
Ditto on Bruno Lorenzon and Vincent Dureuil-Janthial!

Had my first Lorenzon today, Mercurey Le Chapitre 2016 (around 25 EUR all-in). I must say I am impressed. Beautiful balanced nose, red fruits, herbs. I’d go for a mix between Gevrey (depth) and Chambolle (finesse) if I would compare with cote d’or. Decent lenghth, rather soft tannins promising a 10 years evolution curve for my palate. Very drinkable.

When I compare it to (i) an Arnoux-Lachaux Vosne 2015 I had the day before, it has more lenght and depth (nose of the AL was maybe better) and (ii) an series of Gevrey 1er crus 2005 over the weekend, this wine had more silky tannins.

Quite an eye opener (although I must say a Raquillet Mercurey 1er cru les Naugues 2016 last year was also a revelation).
I’d go with PYCM Ancegnieres or the not technically correct answer of Walter Scott X Novo
